--- SOURCE 1: Authoritarianism ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Authoritarianism

SUMMARY:
Competitive authoritarian regimes differ from fully authoritarian regimes in that elections are regularly held, the opposition can openly operate without a high risk of exile or imprisonment and "democratic procedures are sufficiently meaningful for opposition groups to take them seriously as arenas through which to contest for power." Competitive authoritarian regimes lack one or more of the three characteristics of democracies such as free elections (i.e. elections untainted by substantial fraud or voter intimidation); protection of civil liberties (i.e. the freedom of speech, press and association) and an even playing field (in terms of access to resources, the media and legal recourse).

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# Authoritarianism

**Authoritarianism** is a [political system](/wiki/Political_system "Political system") characterized by the rejection of [political plurality](/wiki/Political_plurality "Political plurality"), the use of strong central power to preserve the political *[status quo](/wiki/Status_quo "Status quo")*, and reductions in [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy"), [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers"), [civil liberties](/wiki/Civil_liberties "Civil liberties"), and the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law").[[1]](#cite_note-Kalu2019-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Cerutti-2) Authoritarian [regimes](/wiki/Regime "Regime") may be either [autocratic](/wiki/Autocratic "Autocratic") or [oligarchic](/wiki/Oligarchic "Oligarchic") and may be based upon the rule of a [party](/wiki/Dominant-party_system "Dominant-party system") or the [military](/wiki/Military_dictatorship "Military dictatorship").[[3]](#cite_note-EscrowFrantz-3)[[4]](#cite_note-LaiSlater-4) States that have a blurred boundary between democracy and authoritarianism have sometimes been characterized as "hybrid democracies", "[hybrid regimes](/wiki/Hybrid_regimes "Hybrid regimes")" or "competitive authoritarian" states.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The political scientist [Juan Linz](/wiki/Juan_Linz "Juan Linz"), in an influential[[8]](#cite_note-8) 1964 work, *An Authoritarian Regime: Spain*, defined authoritarianism as possessing four qualities:

Minimally defined, an authoritarian government lacks free and competitive [direct elections](/wiki/Direct_election "Direct election") to [legislatures](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature"), free and competitive direct or [indirect elections](/wiki/Indirect_election "Indirect election") for [executives](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)"), or both.[[11]](#cite_note-Svolik2223-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Broadly defined, authoritarian states include countries that lack [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") such as [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion"), or countries in which the government and the [opposition](/wiki/Opposition_(politics) "Opposition (politics)") do not alternate in power at least once following free elections.[[15]](#cite_note-Svolik20-15) Authoritarian states might contain nominally democratic institutions such as political parties, legislatures and elections which are managed to entrench authoritarian rule and can feature fraudulent, non-competitive elections.[[16]](#cite_note-SvolikNominal-16)

Since 1946, the share of authoritarian states in the international political system increased until the mid-1970s but declined from then until the year 2000.[[17]](#cite_note-Svolik25-17) Prior to 2000, dictatorships typically began with a [coup](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") and replaced a pre-existing authoritarian regime.[[18]](#cite_note-Geddes-2024-18) Since 2000, dictatorships are most likely to begin through democratic backsliding whereby a democratically elected leader established an authoritarian regime.[[18]](#cite_note-Geddes-2024-18)

Authoritarian states usually result in worse health outcomes, a weaker economy and more corruption than in more democratic nations.

## Contents

## Characteristics

Authoritarianism is characterized by highly concentrated and [centralized government](/wiki/Centralized_government "Centralized government") power maintained by [political repression](/wiki/Political_repression "Political repression") and the exclusion of potential or supposed challengers by armed force. It uses [political parties](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") and mass organizations to mobilize people around the goals of the regime.[[19]](#cite_note-Vestal-19) [Adam Przeworski](/wiki/Adam_Przeworski "Adam Przeworski") has theorized that "authoritarian equilibrium rests mainly on lies, fear and economic prosperity."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Authoritarianism also tends to embrace the informal and unregulated exercise of [political power](/wiki/Power_(politics) "Power (politics)"), a leadership that is "self-appointed and even if elected cannot be displaced by citizens' free choice among competitors", the arbitrary deprivation of [civil liberties](/wiki/Civil_liberties "Civil liberties") and little tolerance for meaningful [opposition](/wiki/Opposition_(politics) "Opposition (politics)").[[19]](#cite_note-Vestal-19) A range of [social controls](/wiki/Social_control "Social control") also attempt to stifle [civil society](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society") while political stability is maintained by control over and support of the [armed forces](/wiki/Armed_forces "Armed forces"), a bureaucracy staffed by the regime and creation of [allegiance](/wiki/Allegiance "Allegiance") through various means of [socialization](/wiki/Socialization "Socialization") and indoctrination.[[19]](#cite_note-Vestal-19) [Pippa Norris](/wiki/Pippa_Norris "Pippa Norris") and [Ronald Inglehart](/wiki/Ronald_Inglehart "Ronald Inglehart") identify authoritarianism in politicians and political parties by looking for values of security, conformity, and obedience.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Authoritarianism is marked by "indefinite political tenure" of the ruler or [ruling party](/wiki/Ruling_party "Ruling party") (often in a [one-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state")) or other authority.[[19]](#cite_note-Vestal-19) The transition from an authoritarian system to a more [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") form of government is referred to as [democratization](/wiki/Democratization "Democratization").[[19]](#cite_note-Vestal-19)

### Constitutions in authoritarian regimes

Authoritarian regimes often adopt "the institutional trappings" of democracies such as [constitutions](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution").[[22]](#cite_note-22) Constitutions in authoritarian states may serve a variety of roles, including "operating manual" (describing how the government is to function); "billboard" (signal of regime's intent), "blueprint" (outline of future regime plans), and "window dressing" (material designed to obfuscate, such as provisions setting forth freedoms that are not honored in practice).[[23]](#cite_note-23) Authoritarian constitutions may help legitimize, strengthen, and consolidate regimes.[[24]](#cite_note-24) An authoritarian constitution "that successfully coordinates government action and defines popular expectations can also help consolidate the regime's grip on power by inhibiting re coordination on a different set of arrangements."[[25]](#cite_note-25) Unlike democratic constitutions, authoritarian constitutions do not set direct limits on executive authority; however, in some cases such documents may function as ways for elites to protect their own property rights or constrain autocrats' behavior.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The [Soviet Russia Constitution of 1918](/wiki/Soviet_Russia_Constitution_of_1918 "Soviet Russia Constitution of 1918"), the first charter of the new [Russian Socialist Federated Soviet Republic](/wiki/Russian_Socialist_Federated_Soviet_Republic "Russian Socialist Federated Soviet Republic") (RSFSR), was described by [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") as a "revolutionary" document. It was, he said, unlike any constitution drafted by a nation-state.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The concept of "authoritarian constitutionalism" has been developed by legal scholar [Mark Tushnet](/wiki/Mark_Tushnet "Mark Tushnet").[[28]](#cite_note-Tushnet-28) Tushnet distinguishes authoritarian constitutionalist regimes from "liberal constitutionalist" regimes ("the sort familiar in the modern West, with core commitments to human rights and self-governance implemented by means of varying institutional devices") and from purely authoritarian regimes (which reject the idea of human rights or constraints on leaders' power).[[28]](#cite_note-Tushnet-28) He describes authoritarian constitutionalist regimes as (1) authoritarian [dominant-party](/wiki/Dominant-party_system "Dominant-party system") states that (2) impose sanctions (such as libel judgments) against, but do not [arbitrarily arrest](/wiki/Arbitrary_arrest_and_detention "Arbitrary arrest and detention"), political dissidents; (3) permit "reasonably open discussion and criticism of its policies"; (4) hold "reasonably free and fair elections", without systemic intimidation, but "with close attention to such matters as the drawing of election districts and the creation of party lists to ensure as best it can that it will prevail – and by a substantial margin"; (5) reflect at least occasional responsiveness to public opinion; and (6) create "mechanisms to ensure that the amount of dissent does not exceed the level it regards as desirable." Tushnet cites [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") as an example of an authoritarian constitutionalist state, and connects the concept to that of [hybrid regimes](/wiki/Hybrid_regimes "Hybrid regimes").[[28]](#cite_note-Tushnet-28)

### Economy

Scholars such as [Seymour Lipset](/wiki/Seymour_Martin_Lipset "Seymour Martin Lipset"),[[29]](#cite_note-29) Carles Boix, [Susan Stokes](/wiki/Susan_Stokes "Susan Stokes"),[[30]](#cite_note-30) Dietrich Rueschemeyer, Evelyne Stephens and John Stephens[[31]](#cite_note-31) argue that economic development increases the likelihood of democratization. [Adam Przeworski](/wiki/Adam_Przeworski "Adam Przeworski") and [Fernando Limongi](/wiki/Fernando_Limongi "Fernando Limongi") argue that while economic development makes democracies less likely to turn authoritarian, there is insufficient evidence to conclude that development causes democratization (turning an authoritarian state into a democracy).[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Eva Bellin argues that under certain circumstances the [bourgeoise](/wiki/Bourgeoise "Bourgeoise") and [labor](/wiki/Labour_movement "Labour movement") are more likely to favor democratization, but less so under other circumstances.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Economic development can boost public support for authoritarian regimes in the short-to-medium term.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

According to Michael Albertus, most [land reform programs](/wiki/Land_reform "Land reform") tend to be implemented by authoritarian regimes that subsequently withhold [property rights](/wiki/Right_to_property "Right to property") from the beneficiaries of the land reform. Authoritarian regimes do so to gain coercive leverage over rural populations.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Institutions

Authoritarian regimes typically incorporate similar political institutions to that of democratic regimes, such as legislatures and judiciaries, although they may serve different purposes. Democratic regimes are marked by institutions that are essential to economic development and individual freedom, including representative legislatures and competitive political parties.[[36]](#cite_note-WhatEveryoneNeedstoKnow-36)[[37]](#cite_note-Carnegie-37) Most authoritarian regimes embrace these political structures, but use it in a way that reinforces their power.[[36]](#cite_note-WhatEveryoneNeedstoKnow-36) Authoritarian legislatures, for example, are forums through which leaders may enhance their bases of support, share power, and monitor elites.[[38]](#cite_note-Legislative-38) Additionally, authoritarian party systems are extremely unstable and unconducive to party development, largely due to monopolistic patterns of authority.[[39]](#cite_note-Golosov-39) Judiciaries may be present in authoritarian states where they serve to repress political challengers, institutionalize punishment, and undermine the rule of law.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Democratic and authoritarian arguably differ most prominently in their elections. Democratic elections are generally inclusive, competitive, and fair.[[41]](#cite_note-Kirk-41) In most instances, the elected leader is appointed to act on behalf of the general will. Authoritarian elections, on the other hand, are frequently subject to [fraud](/wiki/Electoral_fraud "Electoral fraud") and extreme constraints on the participation of opposing parties.[[39]](#cite_note-Golosov-39) Autocratic leaders employ tactics like murdering political opposition and paying election monitors to ensure victory.[[36]](#cite_note-WhatEveryoneNeedstoKnow-36)[[42]](#cite_note-Fraud-42) The proportion of authoritarian regimes with elections and support parties has risen in recent years.[[36]](#cite_note-WhatEveryoneNeedstoKnow-36) This is largely due to the increasing popularity of democracies and electoral autocracies, leading authoritarian regimes to imitate democratic regimes in hopes of receiving [foreign aid](/wiki/Aid "Aid") and dodging criticism.[[36]](#cite_note-WhatEveryoneNeedstoKnow-36)[[43]](#cite_note-Data-43) Flawed elections also give authoritarians a controlled way to monitor public sentiment.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

According to a 2018 study, most party-led dictatorships regularly hold popular elections. Prior to the 1990s, most of these elections had no alternative parties or candidates for voters to choose. Since the end of the Cold War, about two-thirds of elections in authoritarian systems allow for some opposition, but the elections are structured in a way to heavily favor the incumbent authoritarian regime.[[45]](#cite_note-Geddes-2018-45) In 2020, almost half of all authoritarian systems had multi-party governments.[[46]](#cite_note-Bokobza-2024-46) Cabinet appointments by an authoritarian regime to outsiders can consolidate their rule by dividing the opposition and co-opting outsiders.[[46]](#cite_note-Bokobza-2024-46)

Hindrances to free and fair elections in authoritarian systems may include:[[45]](#cite_note-Geddes-2018-45)

### Interactions with other elites and the masses

The foundations of stable authoritarian rule are that the authoritarian prevents contestation from the masses and other elites. The authoritarian regime may use co-optation or [repression](/wiki/Political_repression "Political repression") (or carrots and sticks) to prevent revolts.[[47]](#cite_note-Svolik21523-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) Authoritarian rule entails a balancing act whereby the ruler has to maintain the support of other elites (frequently through the distribution of state and societal resources) and the support of the public (through distribution of the same resources): the authoritarian rule is at risk if the balancing act is lopsided, as it risks a coup by the elites or an uprising by the mass public.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Manipulation of information

According to a 2019 study by [Sergei Guriev](/wiki/Sergei_Guriev "Sergei Guriev") and [Daniel Treisman](/wiki/Daniel_Treisman "Daniel Treisman"), authoritarian regimes have over time become less reliant on violence and mass [repression](/wiki/Political_repression "Political repression") to maintain control. The study shows instead that authoritarians have increasingly resorted to [manipulation of information](/wiki/Media_manipulation "Media manipulation") as a means of control. Authoritarians increasingly seek to create an appearance of good performance, conceal state repression, and imitate democracy.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

While authoritarian regimes invest considerably in propaganda out of a belief that it enhances regime survival, scholars have offered mixed views as to whether propaganda is effective.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Systemic weakness and resilience

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Anarchist_placard%2C_2011.jpg/250px-Anarchist_placard%2C_2011.jpg)

[Andrew J. Nathan](/wiki/Andrew_J._Nathan "Andrew J. Nathan") notes that "regime theory holds that authoritarian systems are inherently fragile because of weak legitimacy, overreliance on coercion, over-[centralization](/wiki/Centralization "Centralization") of decision making, and the predominance of personal power over institutional norms. ... Few authoritarian regimes – be they communist, fascist, corporatist, or personalist – have managed to conduct orderly, peaceful, timely, and stable successions."[[53]](#cite_note-NathanAuthoritarian-53)

Political scientist Theodore M. Vestal writes that authoritarian political systems may be weakened through inadequate responsiveness to either popular or elite demands and that the authoritarian tendency to respond to challenges by exerting tighter control, instead of by adapting, may compromise the [legitimacy](/wiki/Legitimacy_(political) "Legitimacy (political)") of an authoritarian state and lead to its collapse.[[19]](#cite_note-Vestal-19)

One exception to this general trend is the endurance of the authoritarian rule of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") which has been unusually resilient among authoritarian regimes. Nathan posits that this can be attributed to four factors such as (1) "the increasingly norm-bound nature of its succession politics"; (2) "the increase in meritocratic as opposed to factional considerations in the promotion of political elites"; (3) "the differentiation and functional specialization of institutions within the regime"; and (4) "the establishment of institutions for political participation and appeal that strengthen the CCP's legitimacy among the public at large."[[53]](#cite_note-NathanAuthoritarian-53)

Some scholars have challenged notions that authoritarian states are inherently brittle systems that require [repression](/wiki/Political_repression "Political repression") and propaganda to make people comply with the authoritarian regime. [Adam Przeworski](/wiki/Adam_Przeworski "Adam Przeworski") has challenged this, noting that while authoritarian regimes do take actions that serve to enhance regime survival, they also engage in mundane everyday governance and their subjects do not hold a posture towards the regime at all moments of their life. He writes, "People in autocracies do not incessantly live under the shadow of dramatic historical events; they lead everyday routine lives."[[54]](#cite_note-54) Similarly, [Thomas Pepinsky](/wiki/Thomas_Pepinsky "Thomas Pepinsky") has challenged the common mental image of an authoritarian state as one of grim totalitarianism, desperate hardship, strict censorship, and dictatorial orders of murder, torture and disappearances. He writes, "life in authoritarian states is mostly boring and tolerable."[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Violence

Yale University political scientist Milan Svolik argues that violence is a common characteristic of authoritarian systems. Violence tends to be common in authoritarian states because of a lack of independent third parties empowered to settle disputes between the dictator, regime allies, regime soldiers and the masses.[[47]](#cite_note-Svolik21523-47)

Authoritarians may resort to measures referred to as *[coup-proofing](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat#Coup-proofing "Coup d'état")* (structures that make it hard for any small group to seize power). Coup-proofing strategies include strategically placing family, ethnic, and religious groups in the military; creating of an armed force parallel to the regular military; and developing multiple internal security agencies with overlapping jurisdiction that constantly monitor one another.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Research shows that some coup-proofing strategies reduce the risk of coups occurring[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) and reduce the likelihood of mass protests.[[59]](#cite_note-59) However, coup-proofing reduces military effectiveness,[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) and limits the rents that an incumbent can extract.[[64]](#cite_note-64) A 2016 study shows that the implementation of [succession rules](/wiki/Order_of_succession "Order of succession") reduce the occurrence of coup attempts.[[65]](#cite_note-Frantz-2016-65) Succession rules are believed to hamper [coordination efforts](/wiki/Coordination_game "Coordination game") among coup plotters by assuaging elites who have more to gain by patience than by plotting.[[65]](#cite_note-Frantz-2016-65) According to political scientists Curtis Bell and Jonathan Powell, coup attempts in neighboring countries lead to greater coup-proofing and coup-related [repression](/wiki/Political_repression "Political repression") in a region.[[66]](#cite_note-66) A 2017 study finds that countries' coup-proofing strategies are heavily influenced by other countries with similar histories.[[67]](#cite_note-67) A 2018 study in the *[Journal of Peace Research](/wiki/Journal_of_Peace_Research "Journal of Peace Research")* found that leaders who survive coup attempts and respond by purging known and potential rivals are likely to have longer tenures as leaders.[[68]](#cite_note-68) A 2019 study in *Conflict Management and Peace Science* found that [personalist](/wiki/Personalist "Personalist") dictatorships are more likely to take coup-proofing measures than other authoritarian regimes; the authors argue that this is because "personalists are characterized by weak institutions and narrow support bases, a lack of unifying ideologies and informal links to the ruler."[[69]](#cite_note-69)

According to a 2019 study, personalist dictatorships are more repressive than other forms of dictatorship.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

## Typologies

According to [Yale](/wiki/Yale "Yale") professor [Juan José Linz](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_Linz "Juan José Linz") there a three main types of political regimes today: [democracies](/wiki/Democracies "Democracies"),
[totalitarian regimes](/wiki/Totalitarian_regimes "Totalitarian regimes") and, sitting between these two, authoritarian regimes (with [hybrid regimes](/wiki/Hybrid_regime "Hybrid regime")).[[71]](#cite_note-LinzLinz2000-71)[[72]](#cite_note-Michie2014-72)

According to University of Michigan professor Dan Slater, modern forms of authoritarianism are fundamentally dissimilar from historical forms of nondemocratic rule. He links modern authoritarianism to the era of [mass politics](/wiki/Mass_politics "Mass politics"), which began with the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution").[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### Similar terms

### Subtypes

Several subtypes of authoritarian regimes have been identified by Linz and others.[[82]](#cite_note-Gasiorowski-82) Linz identified the two most basic subtypes as traditional authoritarian regimes and bureaucratic-military authoritarian regimes:

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Card_Stunt_for_Park_Chung-hee.jpg/250px-Card_Stunt_for_Park_Chung-hee.jpg)

According to [Barbara Geddes](/wiki/Barbara_Geddes "Barbara Geddes"), there are seven typologies of authoritarian regimes: dominant party regimes, military regime, personalist regimes, monarchies, oligarchic regimes, indirect military regimes, or hybrids of the first three.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

Subtypes of authoritarian regimes identified by Linz are [corporatist](/wiki/Corporatism "Corporatism") or organic-statistic, racial and ethnic "democracy" and post-totalitarian.[[82]](#cite_note-Gasiorowski-82)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/18th_Summit_of_Non-Aligned_Movement_gets_underway_in_Baku_026.jpg/250px-18th_Summit_of_Non-Aligned_Movement_gets_underway_in_Baku_026.jpg)

Authoritarian regimes are also sometimes subcategorized by whether they are more personalistic or [populist](/wiki/Populism "Populism").[[82]](#cite_note-Gasiorowski-82)[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Personalistic authoritarian regimes are characterized by arbitrary rule and [authority](/wiki/Authority "Authority") exercised "mainly through patronage networks and coercion rather than through institutions and formal rules."[[82]](#cite_note-Gasiorowski-82) Personalistic authoritarian regimes have been seen in post-colonial Africa. By contrast, populist authoritarian regimes "are mobilizational regimes in which a strong, charismatic, manipulative leader rules through a coalition involving key lower-class groups."[[82]](#cite_note-Gasiorowski-82) Examples include [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") under [Juan Perón](/wiki/Juan_Per%C3%B3n "Juan Perón"),[[82]](#cite_note-Gasiorowski-82) [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") under [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") under [Gamal Abdel Nasser](/wiki/Gamal_Abdel_Nasser "Gamal Abdel Nasser")[[82]](#cite_note-Gasiorowski-82) and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela") under [Hugo Chávez](/wiki/Hugo_Ch%C3%A1vez "Hugo Chávez") and [Nicolás Maduro](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Maduro "Nicolás Maduro").[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)

A typology of authoritarian regimes by political scientists Brian Lai and Dan Slater includes four categories:

Lai and Slater argue that single-party regimes are better than military regimes at developing institutions (e.g. [mass mobilization](/wiki/Mass_mobilization "Mass mobilization"), patronage networks and coordination of elites) that are effective at continuing the regime's incumbency and diminishing domestic challengers; Lai and Slater also argue that military regimes more often initiate military conflicts or undertake other "desperate measures" to maintain control as compared to single-party regimes.[[4]](#cite_note-LaiSlater-4)[[3]](#cite_note-EscrowFrantz-3)

John Duckitt suggests a link between authoritarianism and [collectivism](/wiki/Collectivism_and_individualism "Collectivism and individualism"), asserting that both stand in opposition to [individualism](/wiki/Individualism "Individualism").[[92]](#cite_note-92) Duckitt writes that both authoritarianism and collectivism submerge [individual rights](/wiki/Individual_rights "Individual rights") and goals to group goals, expectations and [conformities](/wiki/Conformity "Conformity").[[93]](#cite_note-93)

According to Steven Levitsky and Lucan Way, authoritarian regimes that are created in [social revolutions](/wiki/Social_revolution "Social revolution") are far more durable than other kinds of authoritarian regimes.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Authoritarianism and democracy

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Economist_Intelligence_Unit_Democracy_Index_2024.svg/500px-Economist_Intelligence_Unit_Democracy_Index_2024.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/V-Dem_Electoral_Democracy_Index_2025_Robinson_World.svg/500px-V-Dem_Electoral_Democracy_Index_2025_Robinson_World.svg.png)

Authoritarianism and democracy are not necessarily fundamental opposites and may be thought of as poles at opposite ends of a scale, so that it is possible for some democracies to possess authoritarian elements, and for an authoritarian system to have democratic elements.[[96]](#cite_note-96)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*][[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Authoritarian regimes may also be partly responsive to citizen grievances, although this is generally only regarding grievances that do not undermine the stability of the regime.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100) An [illiberal democracy](/wiki/Illiberal_democracy "Illiberal democracy"), or [procedural democracy](/wiki/Procedural_democracy "Procedural democracy"), is distinguished from [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy"), or [substantive democracy](/wiki/Substantive_democracy "Substantive democracy"), in that illiberal democracies lack features such as the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law"), protections for [minority groups](/wiki/Minority_group "Minority group"), an [independent judiciary](/wiki/Judicial_independence "Judicial independence") and the real [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers").[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)

A further distinction that liberal democracies have rarely made war with one another; research has extended the theory and finds that more democratic countries tend to have few wars (sometimes called [militarized interstate disputes](/wiki/Militarized_interstate_dispute "Militarized interstate dispute")) causing fewer battle deaths with one another and that democracies have far fewer [civil wars](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war").[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106)

Research shows that the democratic nations have much less [democide](/wiki/Democide "Democide") or murder by government. Those were also moderately developed nations before applying liberal democratic policies.[[107]](#cite_note-107) Research by the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") suggests that political institutions are extremely important in determining the prevalence of [corruption](/wiki/Political_corruption "Political corruption") and that parliamentary systems, political stability and [freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press") are all associated with lower corruption.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

A 2006 study by economist [Alberto Abadie](/wiki/Alberto_Abadie "Alberto Abadie") has concluded that terrorism is most common in nations with intermediate [political freedom](/wiki/Political_freedom "Political freedom"). The nations with the least [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism") are the most and least democratic nations, and that "transitions from an authoritarian regime to a democracy may be accompanied by temporary increases in terrorism."[[109]](#cite_note-109) Studies in 2013 and 2017 similarly found a nonlinear relationship between political freedom and terrorism, with the most terrorist attacks occurring in partial democra

# Competitive Authoritarianism

Competitive authoritarianism is a kind of hybrid regime, or a political regime that combines elements of both democracy and authoritarianism. In competitive authoritarian regimes, multiparty elections are held regularly and are hard-fought affairs. However, the “playing field” is uneven: control of the media, abuse of state resources, coercion, and/or limited fraud give incumbents an unfair advantage over the opposition. Because elections are not free and fair, these regimes are not democratic. Yet neither are they fully authoritarian. Although unfair, they are not totally meaningless. In competitive authoritarian regimes, there is real competition and some uncertainty regarding the outcome of elections.

### Hybrid Regimes

The concept of competitive authoritarianism was developed by Steven Levitsky and Lucan Way to describe a [Page 123]kind of hybrid regime that became increasingly common after the cold war. To be considered a democracy, a regime must possess four key attributes: free and fair elections, full adult suffrage, protection of a broad range of civil liberties, and the absence of unelected “tutelary” bodies that restrict elected governments’ ability to govern. This is called the “procedural minimum definition of democracy.” Some regimes possess most of these attributes but not all; these are “hybrid regimes,” of which there are various kinds. For example, an otherwise-democratic regime in which there is not full adult suffrage is a constitutional oligarchy. An otherwise democratic regime in which the military restricts the ability of elected governments to govern is a tutelary regime.[Page 124]According to Levitsky and Way, it is necessary to add a fifth attribute to the procedural minimum definition of democracy: the existence of a reasonably level playing field between incumbents and opposition. An otherwise-democratic regime in which the playing field is uneven is a competitive authoritarian regime.

There are various tools that incumbents in competitive authoritarian regimes can use to tilt the playing field in their favor. One common tool is control of the media, especially television. Another is abuse of state resources: incumbents may use public finances to fund their campaigns, deploy the machinery of the state (buildings, vehicles, public employees, etc.) on their behalf, or use the state’s distributive and regulatory powers to reward supporters and punish opponents. A third common tool is coercion, ranging from the selective use of “legal” repression, such as libel laws, to threats and physical violence. Finally, incumbents may use limited fraud. While Levitsky and Way specify that fraud cannot be so massive as to make elections meaningless (when this occurs, the regime is fully authoritarian, not competitive authoritarian), incumbents may use limited to fraud to secure victory.

### Competitive Authoritarianism After the Cold War

The post–cold war period saw a proliferation of competitive authoritarian regimes. With the fall of the Soviet Union and the emergence of the United States as the world’s only superpower, political elites in developing and postcommunist countries faced pressure to adopt the outward forms of democracy. Yet adopting the forms was not the same as adopting the substance: many countries began to hold multiparty elections, but these were not free and fair. While observers noted the shortcomings of these regimes, they often described them optimistically as being in the midst of prolonged transitions to democracy.

--- SOURCE 5: The Concept of "Competitive Authoritarianism" ---
URL: https://www.reddit.com/r/PoliticalScience/comments/1lpzf93/the_concept_of_competitive_authoritarianism/

SUMMARY:
In a competitive authoritarian system, a leader comes to power democratically and then erodes the system of checks and balances.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Competitive authoritarian regimes hold multiparty elections regularly, creating an illusion of democracy despite systemic biases favoring incumbents and undermining genuine electoral competition.

While opposition parties can operate, their effectiveness is severely undermined by state repression, media control, and resource allocation that favors ruling parties, restricting genuine competition.
Incumbents manipulate media narratives through state control and censorship, ensuring favorable coverage while suppressing dissenting voices, thus shaping public perception to maintain power.
State resources are often misappropriated to support ruling parties, using public funds for campaign activities, which skews the electoral process and reinforces incumbents' advantages.
Authoritarian regimes employ coercive tactics, including intimidation, legal harassment, and violence against opposition figures, to deter dissent and maintain dominance over political discourse.
